q: What does ACID stand for, and what does each property guarantee?
a: ACID is the set of guarantees a relational transaction provides. Atomicity: all statements in a transaction succeed or none do (no partial commits). Consistency: a committed transaction moves the database from one valid state to another, preserving constraints, foreign keys, and triggers. Isolation: concurrent transactions behave as if they ran one at a time, to the degree the isolation level promises. Durability: once COMMIT returns, the change survives crashes (typically via a write-ahead log flushed to disk).
fs: Atomicity and Durability are about a single transaction surviving failures; Isolation and Consistency are about correctness under concurrency and constraints.
fj: Thinking ACID just means 'the database is reliable' without being able to name what each letter actually guarantees.

q: What is a database transaction, and why wrap multiple statements in one?
a: A transaction is a unit of work that is committed or rolled back as a whole. You wrap related statements (e.g., debit one account, credit another) so the database never persists a half-finished operation. If any statement fails or you ROLLBACK, every change in the transaction is undone. Without a transaction, a crash between the two UPDATEs could leave money debited but never credited.
fs: Group statements that must succeed or fail together; the classic test is the bank transfer where a partial result is a corrupt state.
fj: Assuming each statement is automatically safe and never considering what happens if the process dies between two writes.

q: Name the four SQL standard isolation levels and the read anomaly each one prevents.
a: From weakest to strongest: READ UNCOMMITTED allows dirty reads (seeing another transaction's uncommitted data). READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads. REPEATABLE READ prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads but the standard allows phantom reads. SERIALIZABLE prevents all three. Each step up forbids one more anomaly: dirty read, then non-repeatable read, then phantom read.
fs: The ladder maps cleanly: dirty read -> non-repeatable read -> phantom read, each level forbidding one more anomaly.
fj: Confusing the order or thinking a higher isolation level is strictly better, ignoring the concurrency and retry costs.

q: What is the difference between a non-repeatable read and a phantom read?
a: A non-repeatable read is when you read a row, another transaction commits an UPDATE/DELETE to that same row, and re-reading it returns different data. A phantom read is when you run a range query (e.g., WHERE age > 30), another transaction commits an INSERT/DELETE that matches that predicate, and re-running the query returns a different set of rows. Non-repeatable is about an existing row changing; phantom is about the membership of a result set changing.
fs: Non-repeatable read = an individual row's values change; phantom read = the row count for a predicate changes due to inserts or deletes.
fj: Treating the two as the same thing, or assuming REPEATABLE READ alone always blocks phantoms in every engine.

q: What is the default isolation level in PostgreSQL, and what surprises people about its REPEATABLE READ?
a: PostgreSQL defaults to READ COMMITTED, where each statement sees a fresh snapshot taken at statement start, so two SELECTs in one transaction can see different committed data. PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ takes a single snapshot at transaction start and, unlike the bare SQL standard, also prevents phantom reads. Its SERIALIZABLE adds Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI), which detects dependency cycles and aborts a transaction with a serialization error you must retry.
fs: Postgres READ COMMITTED is per-statement snapshots; its REPEATABLE READ is snapshot isolation that already blocks phantoms, stronger than the standard requires.
fj: Assuming the default is SERIALIZABLE, or that READ COMMITTED gives a stable snapshot for the whole transaction.

q: What problem does MVCC solve, and how does PostgreSQL implement it?
a: MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) lets readers and writers avoid blocking each other by keeping multiple versions of a row. In PostgreSQL, an UPDATE does not overwrite in place; it writes a new row version and marks the old one obsolete. Each row carries xmin (the txid that created it) and xmax (the txid that deleted/replaced it). A transaction compares these against its snapshot to decide visibility, so reads never wait on writes. The cost is dead tuples that VACUUM must later reclaim.
fs: MVCC trades storage and vacuuming for non-blocking reads; visibility is decided by comparing per-row xmin/xmax against the transaction's snapshot.
fj: Believing the database edits rows in place and that a long-running reader must therefore block writers.

q: Under MVCC, do readers block writers or writers block readers?
a: No. The whole point of MVCC is that ordinary reads never block writes and writes never block reads, because each reader sees a consistent snapshot of older row versions while a writer creates new versions. Two writers touching the same row still conflict: the second waits for the first to commit or roll back (row-level write lock). So write-write contention remains, but read-write contention is largely eliminated.
fs: Read-write contention disappears under MVCC; only write-write on the same row still serializes via row locks.
fj: Claiming MVCC removes all locking, or that a SELECT can block an UPDATE on the same row.

q: How does a B-tree index work, and why is it the default index type?
a: A B-tree (really a B+-tree in most engines) is a balanced, sorted tree where leaf nodes hold key values in order and point to rows. Lookups, inserts, and deletes are O(log n) because the tree stays balanced. Because keys are stored sorted, a B-tree serves equality (=), range (<, >, BETWEEN), ORDER BY, and prefix LIKE 'abc%' queries. That generality is why it is the default index in PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, and most systems.
fs: B-trees keep keys sorted and balanced, so one structure handles equality, ranges, sorting, and prefix matches in O(log n).
fj: Thinking any index makes any query fast, without knowing the sorted structure is what enables range and ORDER BY support.

code.label: B-tree serves range
lines: -- range + ORDER BY both use it

q: When would a hash index beat a B-tree index, and what can it not do?
a: A hash index stores a hash of the key, giving roughly O(1) average lookups for exact equality (= or IN). For pure equality on a high-cardinality column with many point lookups, it can be faster and smaller than a B-tree. Its limitation is fundamental: because hashing destroys ordering, it cannot serve range queries (<, >, BETWEEN), ORDER BY, or prefix LIKE. In practice B-trees are preferred because they handle equality well too and are far more flexible.
fs: Hash indexes win only for high-volume exact-match lookups; they are useless for ranges, sorting, or prefixes because hashing throws away order.
fj: Reaching for a hash index by default, not realizing it cannot do range scans or ordering.

q: How do you read an EXPLAIN / query plan, and what does EXPLAIN ANALYZE add?
a: EXPLAIN shows the planner's chosen execution tree (scans, joins, sorts) with estimated cost, row counts, and access methods, without running the query. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually executes it and reports real timings and actual row counts, so you can compare estimated vs actual rows. A big gap between estimated and actual rows usually means stale statistics or a bad selectivity estimate. You read the tree from the innermost/most-indented node outward, since children feed their parents.
fs: Compare estimated vs actual rows in EXPLAIN ANALYZE; large divergence points to stale stats driving a bad plan.
fj: Reading only the total cost number and never checking whether estimated rows match actual rows.

code.label: See real vs estimated
lines: -- look for Seq Scan vs Index Scan

q: You see a Seq Scan in the plan where you expected an Index Scan. What could explain it?
a: Several reasons. The query may match a large fraction of the table, so a sequential scan is genuinely cheaper than many random index lookups. Statistics may be stale, so the planner misestimates selectivity (run ANALYZE). The predicate may not be sargable, e.g., wrapping the column in a function (WHERE lower(email) = ...) or a leading-wildcard LIKE '%foo' prevents index use. Or no suitable index exists, or an implicit type cast on the column defeats it.
fs: Seq scan is correct when selectivity is low; the bugs are stale stats, non-sargable predicates, or function-wrapped columns.
fj: Assuming a sequential scan always means a missing index, and immediately adding indexes that the planner then ignores.

q: What is database normalization, and what do 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF require?
a: Normalization organizes columns and tables to reduce redundancy and update anomalies. 1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups or arrays in a column. 2NF: 1NF plus every non-key column depends on the whole composite key, not just part of it. 3NF: 2NF plus no transitive dependencies, i.e., non-key columns depend only on the key, not on other non-key columns. The payoff is each fact stored once, so updates touch one place.
fs: 3NF means every non-key column depends on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key; the goal is eliminating update anomalies.
fj: Reciting 'normalization removes duplicates' without being able to state what distinguishes 2NF from 3NF.

q: When would you deliberately denormalize, and what do you trade away?
a: You denormalize when read performance and query simplicity matter more than write efficiency, typically in analytics/reporting or hot read paths where joins are too expensive. You duplicate data (e.g., store a customer_name on the orders table, or pre-aggregate totals) to avoid joins. The trade-off is that the same fact now lives in multiple places, so writes must update all copies and you risk inconsistency; you accept that complexity in exchange for faster reads.
fs: Denormalize to trade write complexity and redundancy for faster, simpler reads, common in OLAP star schemas and read-heavy paths.
fj: Denormalizing prematurely for convenience and then silently letting copies drift out of sync.

q: What is a deadlock, and how does the database detect and resolve one?
a: A deadlock is a cycle where transaction A holds a lock B wants while B holds a lock A wants, so neither can proceed. The database runs a deadlock detector that builds a wait-for graph (an edge from each waiting transaction to the one it waits on) and looks for a cycle. When it finds one, it cannot wait it out, so it chooses a victim and aborts that transaction with a deadlock error, breaking the cycle. The victim's application is expected to retry.
fs: Deadlock detection is cycle-finding in a wait-for graph; the engine resolves it by aborting a victim, so apps must be retry-safe.
fj: Believing the database will eventually 'untangle' a deadlock on its own without aborting anyone.

q: How do you prevent deadlocks in application code?
a: The most effective rule is consistent lock ordering: always acquire locks on resources in the same global order (e.g., always lock the lower account id before the higher), so a cycle can never form. Also keep transactions short to shrink the lock-holding window, touch fewer rows per transaction, use a single statement where possible, and add retry-with-backoff for the rare deadlock that still occurs. Lowering isolation level where safe also reduces lock contention.
fs: Order lock acquisition consistently and keep transactions short; combine with idempotent retry for the residual cases.
fj: Sprinkling more locking or higher isolation to 'be safe,' which usually increases deadlock frequency instead.

q: What is the difference between an optimistic and a pessimistic locking strategy?
a: Pessimistic locking assumes conflicts are likely, so it locks rows up front (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) and holds them until commit, blocking other writers. Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare, takes no lock, and instead checks at write time that the row has not changed since you read it, usually with a version column or timestamp; if it changed, the update affects zero rows and you retry. Pessimistic suits high contention; optimistic suits low contention and avoids holding locks.
fs: Pessimistic locks early and blocks; optimistic detects the conflict at commit via a version check and retries, ideal for low-contention workloads.
fj: Assuming you must always SELECT FOR UPDATE, never considering a version-column compare-and-set instead.

q: What is the difference between table partitioning and sharding?
a: Both split a large dataset into pieces, but scope differs. Partitioning divides one table into parts (partitions) typically within a single database/server, e.g., by date range or hash, to make scans and maintenance cheaper while the engine still presents one logical table. Sharding is horizontal partitioning across multiple independent database servers, each shard holding a subset of rows on its own machine. Partitioning scales a single node's manageability; sharding scales out writes and storage beyond one machine.
fs: Partitioning splits a table within one server; sharding spreads partitions across independent servers to scale writes and storage horizontally.
fj: Using 'sharding' and 'partitioning' interchangeably, missing that sharding is specifically across separate machines.

q: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical partitioning?
a: Horizontal partitioning splits a table by rows: each partition has the same columns but a disjoint subset of rows (e.g., orders from 2025 vs 2026, or users hashed by id). Vertical partitioning splits by columns: you move some columns into a separate table (e.g., keep hot, frequently read columns in one table and large blob columns in another) joined by the key. Horizontal addresses row volume and distribution; vertical addresses wide rows and access-pattern separation.
fs: Horizontal = same schema, different rows; vertical = same rows, different columns split by access pattern.
fj: Thinking partitioning is only ever by rows, and not knowing vertical splits columns.

q: How do you choose a shard key, and what goes wrong with a bad one?
a: A good shard key spreads both data and traffic evenly and keeps related rows that are queried together on the same shard. A bad choice causes hotspots: a low-cardinality or monotonically increasing key (like an auto-increment id or current timestamp) sends most new writes to one shard. It also forces cross-shard 'scatter-gather' queries and expensive rebalancing. You also lose easy cross-shard transactions and unique constraints, so the key should match your dominant query and write pattern.
fs: Pick a high-cardinality key aligned to your access pattern; monotonic or low-cardinality keys create hotspots and scatter-gather queries.
fj: Sharding on an auto-increment id or timestamp, which funnels all new writes into the newest shard.

q: What is database replication, and what is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous?
a: Replication keeps copies of the data on multiple servers, usually one primary that takes writes and replicas that receive changes. In synchronous replication, the primary does not acknowledge a commit until at least one replica has confirmed it, so no committed data is lost on failover, at the cost of higher write latency. In asynchronous replication, the primary commits and acknowledges immediately and ships changes later, giving lower latency but a window of possible data loss (replication lag) if the primary fails.
fs: Synchronous trades write latency for zero data loss on failover; asynchronous trades a data-loss window for lower latency.
fj: Assuming replicas are always perfectly up to date, ignoring asynchronous replication lag.

q: How do read replicas help scaling, and what is their main pitfall?
a: Read replicas are copies that serve read queries, letting you spread read load across many servers and offload reporting from the primary. They scale reads and storage redundancy, but they do not increase write throughput, since all writes still go to the single primary. The main pitfall is replication lag with asynchronous replication: a client that writes then immediately reads from a replica may not see its own write (read-after-write inconsistency). You mitigate with read-your-writes routing to the primary or bounded-staleness checks.
fs: Read replicas scale reads but not writes, and asynchronous lag breaks read-your-writes unless you route fresh reads to the primary.
fj: Expecting read replicas to also speed up writes, and being surprised when a just-written row is missing on a replica.

q: What is the difference between OLTP and OLAP workloads?
a: OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) handles many small, short, concurrent read/write transactions, like placing an order or updating a profile; it favors normalized schemas, row-oriented storage, and indexes for fast point lookups. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) handles fewer, long-running analytical queries that scan and aggregate huge volumes, like dashboards and reports; it favors denormalized star schemas and columnar storage. You typically keep them on separate systems so heavy analytics do not starve transactional traffic.
fs: OLTP is many short point operations on normalized row stores; OLAP is few large scans/aggregations on denormalized columnar stores.
fj: Running heavy analytical aggregations directly against the production OLTP database and degrading transaction latency.

q: Why is columnar storage so much faster than row storage for analytical queries?
a: OLAP queries usually read a few columns across millions of rows and aggregate them. Columnar storage keeps each column's values together on disk, so a query like SUM(amount) reads only the amount column instead of every byte of every row. That cuts I/O dramatically and, because a column holds similar values, it compresses far better (run-length, dictionary encoding) and is vectorization-friendly. Row storage is better for OLTP, where you read or write whole rows at a time.
fs: Columnar reads only the touched columns and compresses similar values together, slashing I/O for wide-scan aggregations.
fj: Assuming column vs row layout is just an implementation detail with no real performance impact for analytics.

q: What is a covering index, and how does it speed up a query?
a: A covering index is one that contains every column a query needs, so the engine can answer the query from the index alone without going back to the table (an index-only scan). For example, an index on (customer_id, status) covers SELECT status FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 7. In PostgreSQL you can add non-key payload columns with INCLUDE; in MySQL/InnoDB the secondary index already carries the primary key. The win is avoiding the extra random heap/table lookups per matched row.
fs: A covering index lets the query be answered index-only, eliminating per-row heap lookups for the matched rows.
fj: Indexing only the WHERE column and not realizing the engine still does a table lookup for the selected columns.

q: What is a partial index, and when does it save you significant space and write cost?
a: A partial index covers only the rows matching a WHERE predicate, so it indexes a subset of the table instead of every row. The classic case is a low-cardinality flag where you only ever query the rare value: indexing WHERE status = 'pending' on an orders table that is 99% 'shipped' makes the index tiny and skips maintenance for the common rows. The planner uses it only when the query's predicate is implied by the index's predicate. You get a smaller index, faster lookups, and cheaper writes for the excluded rows.
fs: Index only the rows you actually query (e.g. WHERE status='pending'); the index stays small and writes on excluded rows skip it entirely.
fj: Indexing the whole column even though queries only ever touch a tiny fraction of the rows.

q: When do you reach for a GIN or GiST index instead of a B-tree in PostgreSQL?
a: B-trees index a single scalar key, but GIN and GiST index composite values like full-text documents, JSONB, arrays, and ranges where one row maps to many searchable elements. GIN is an inverted index: it stores an entry per element (lexeme, array member, JSONB key) pointing at matching rows, so it is fast for containment and full-text @@ queries. GiST is a lossy, extensible tree that excels at geometric, range, and nearest-neighbor searches and is cheaper to update. Rule of thumb: GIN for static text/JSONB (faster lookups, slower builds), GiST for dynamic or geometric/range data.
fs: GIN is an inverted index for full-text/JSONB/array containment; GiST suits geometric, range, and nearest-neighbor queries and updates faster.
fj: Trying to put a B-tree on a JSONB or tsvector column and wondering why containment queries still seq scan.

q: What is the N+1 query problem, and how do you fix it?
a: N+1 happens when you run one query to fetch a list of N parent rows, then fire one more query per parent to load its children, for N+1 round trips total. It usually hides behind an ORM's lazy loading: iterating users and accessing user.orders triggers a separate SELECT each loop. The cost is latency, since each round trip pays network and planning overhead. You fix it by fetching the children in one query, either with a JOIN or an IN (...) batch (the ORM's eager/prefetch loading), turning N+1 queries into one or two.
fs: Replace per-row child fetches with a single JOIN or batched IN query; in ORMs that means eager/prefetch loading instead of lazy access in a loop.
fj: Looping over parents and lazily accessing each one's relation, never noticing the hundreds of identical queries in the logs.

q: What is the write-ahead log (WAL), and how does it give you durability?
a: The WAL is an append-only sequential log of every change, written and fsync'd to disk before the change is applied to the actual data pages. That ordering is the whole trick: COMMIT only returns after the WAL record is durably on disk, so even if the process crashes before the dirty data pages are flushed, recovery replays the WAL to reconstruct the committed state. Sequential log appends are far cheaper than random data-page writes, so WAL also batches the real page writes into periodic checkpoints. It is the basis of durability, crash recovery, and physical replication.
fs: Log the change durably before touching data pages; on crash you replay the WAL from the last checkpoint to recover committed transactions.
fj: Assuming COMMIT means the table's data files were written to disk, rather than just the WAL record.

q: In PostgreSQL, where does table bloat come from and what does autovacuum do about it?
a: Because MVCC never overwrites in place, every UPDATE and DELETE leaves a dead tuple, an old row version no live transaction can see. Those dead tuples accumulate as bloat, wasting disk and slowing scans. VACUUM reclaims them by marking that space reusable within the table (it does not return space to the OS without VACUUM FULL). Autovacuum is the background daemon that triggers VACUUM once dead tuples exceed a threshold, by default about 50 rows plus 20% of the table. High-churn tables often need a lower scale factor so vacuum runs more often and bloat stays bounded.
fs: Dead tuples from MVCC UPDATE/DELETE cause bloat; autovacuum reclaims them past a threshold (~50 + 20%), tunable lower for high-churn tables.
fj: Thinking UPDATE replaces a row in place, so a heavily updated table can't bloat and never needs vacuuming.

q: Compare range, list, and hash partitioning. When does each fit?
a: All three split one logical table into physical partitions by a partition key. Range partitioning assigns rows to a partition by a value interval, ideal for time-series where you partition by month and can drop an old partition instantly instead of a slow DELETE. List partitioning maps explicit key values to partitions, good for a bounded set like region or tenant tier. Hash partitioning distributes rows across N partitions by a hash of the key, used to spread load evenly when there is no natural range or list and you mainly want balanced sizes.
fs: Range for time-series (cheap partition drops), list for discrete categories, hash for even distribution when no natural grouping exists.
fj: Defaulting to hash partitioning for time-series data, losing the cheap drop-old-partition retention story range gives you.

q: What is a materialized view, and how is it different from a regular view?
a: A regular view is just a stored query that re-executes every time you read it, so it is always fresh but pays full compute on each access. A materialized view physically stores the query result on disk, so reads are fast like a table, but the data is a snapshot that goes stale until you refresh it. You trade freshness for read speed, which is worth it for expensive aggregations queried far more often than the underlying data changes. The cost is the refresh: a plain REFRESH rebuilds and locks, while CONCURRENTLY refreshes without blocking reads but needs a unique index.
fs: A materialized view caches the query result on disk for fast reads but goes stale until refreshed; a plain view recomputes every read and is always fresh.
fj: Treating a materialized view as auto-updating, then serving stale numbers because nothing ever refreshed it.

code.label: Non-blocking refresh
lines: -- needs a UNIQUE index to do this:

q: How do you run a schema migration with zero downtime, like renaming or splitting a column?
a: Never rename or drop in one step while old code is live; use the expand-contract (parallel-change) pattern. Expand: add the new column or table, additive and backward-compatible, so old code is unaffected. Migrate: backfill historical rows in small batches and deploy code that dual-writes to both old and new. Contract: once the new column is fully populated and all running code uses it, drop the old one, which is a fast metadata-only operation. Each phase is independently deployable and reversible, so no single deploy breaks running instances.
fs: Expand (add new, additive), migrate (batched backfill + dual-write), then contract (drop old) as separate safe deploys; never rename-in-place with old code live.
fj: Doing ALTER TABLE RENAME COLUMN in one migration and breaking every app instance still running the old code.

q: Why do you need a connection pooler like PgBouncer in front of PostgreSQL?
a: Each PostgreSQL backend is a separate OS process costing several MB of RAM, so the server caps out at a few hundred connections, while a fleet of app servers or serverless functions may open thousands. A pooler multiplexes many client connections onto a small set of reused server connections, eliminating per-request connect/teardown overhead and protecting the database from connection exhaustion. PgBouncer's transaction mode hands a server connection to a client only for the duration of a transaction, maximizing reuse, but it breaks session features like prepared statements, SET, and LISTEN/NOTIFY; session mode keeps those but reuses less.
fs: A pooler multiplexes thousands of clients onto a few reused backends; transaction mode maximizes reuse but disallows session state like prepared statements.
fj: Letting every app instance and lambda open its own direct connection until Postgres hits its connection limit and rejects new clients.

q: Walk me through the four NoSQL data models and when each one fits.
a: Key-value stores (Redis, DynamoDB) map an opaque key to a blob for the fastest possible point lookups, ideal for caches, sessions, and feature flags. Document stores (MongoDB) hold self-describing JSON-like documents and suit flexible, evolving schemas where one entity is fetched whole. Wide-column stores (Cassandra, HBase) organize rows into column families and scale writes massively across nodes, designed query-first around known access patterns. Graph databases (Neo4j) make nodes and edges first-class so multi-hop relationship traversals are cheap, fitting social graphs, fraud rings, and recommendations.
fs: Key-value for caches/sessions, document for whole-entity flexible schemas, wide-column for write-heavy known-pattern scale, graph for multi-hop relationship traversals.
fj: Treating 'NoSQL' as one thing and picking a document store for a problem that is really a graph traversal or a simple cache.

q: How does the CAP theorem apply when choosing or operating a distributed database?
a: CAP says that when a network partition splits the nodes, a distributed datastore must choose between consistency (every read sees the latest write) and availability (every request gets a non-error response); it cannot have both during the partition. Partitions are not optional in a real network, so the real decision is your behavior when one happens. A CP system (e.g. a strongly-consistent store) refuses or blocks requests on the minority side to avoid stale reads; an AP system (e.g. Dynamo-style) keeps serving and reconciles later. With no partition you still tune the latency-vs-consistency tradeoff, which is the PACELC extension.
fs: CAP only bites during a partition: pick CP (reject/block to stay consistent) or AP (stay available, reconcile later); PACELC adds the latency tradeoff when there's no partition.
fj: Calling a database 'CA' as if you could permanently opt out of network partitions.

q: What does eventual consistency actually mean, and what bugs does it introduce?
a: Eventual consistency guarantees that if writes stop, all replicas will converge to the same value, but it makes no promise about when, so reads can return stale or out-of-order data in the meantime. Practically this surfaces as read-your-writes violations (you save a profile, reload, and see the old value off a lagging replica) and as conflicting concurrent writes that need a resolution rule like last-write-wins or CRDTs. You design around it with session guarantees (read-your-writes, monotonic reads) by pinning a user to a replica or routing fresh reads to the leader, and by making writes idempotent.
fs: Replicas converge eventually but reads can be stale or reordered until then; mitigate with read-your-writes/monotonic-read session guarantees and idempotent writes.
fj: Assuming a write is instantly visible everywhere and being surprised when a just-saved value reads back as the old one.

q: What is two-phase commit, and what is its fundamental weakness?
a: Two-phase commit (2PC) coordinates an atomic transaction across multiple databases via a coordinator. Phase 1 (prepare): the coordinator asks every participant to prepare and durably promise it can commit; each votes yes or no. Phase 2 (commit/abort): if all voted yes the coordinator tells everyone to commit, otherwise to abort. Its fundamental weakness is blocking: if the coordinator crashes after participants have voted yes but before sending the decision, those participants are stuck holding locks in the prepared state until it recovers. That single point of failure and lock-holding is why many systems prefer sagas or idempotent eventual-consistency patterns instead.
fs: 2PC's prepare/commit gives cross-database atomicity but blocks: a coordinator crash after the yes votes leaves participants holding locks in the prepared state.
fj: Reaching for distributed 2PC across microservices without accounting for the coordinator-failure blocking and lock-holding it causes.

q: Compare hash-based, range-based, and directory-based sharding strategies.
a: Hash sharding assigns a row to a shard by hashing its key, giving even distribution and no hotspots, but range scans must scatter-gather across all shards and resharding reshuffles most keys (consistent hashing reduces that). Range sharding keeps key order across shards, so range queries hit one or few shards, but a monotonic key (timestamp, auto-increment id) hotspots the newest shard. Directory sharding keeps an explicit lookup table mapping keys to shards, giving full placement flexibility and easy rebalancing at the cost of an extra lookup and a directory that must stay highly available.
fs: Hash = even spread but scatter-gather range scans; range = locality but hotspots on monotonic keys; directory = flexible placement at the cost of a lookup table you must keep available.
fj: Picking range sharding on a timestamp key for write-heavy data, then funneling all new writes into one hot shard.

q: What is a foreign key, and what does a database do to enforce referential integrity?
a: A foreign key constrains a column to reference an existing primary/unique key in another table, so the database guarantees no orphan rows: you cannot insert a child pointing at a missing parent, and the engine controls what happens when the parent is deleted via ON DELETE RESTRICT, CASCADE, or SET NULL. Enforcement has a cost: each referencing write checks the parent, and the parent side typically needs an index to make that check and cascading deletes fast. Some high-scale or sharded systems drop FKs and enforce integrity in the application, trading guarantees for write throughput and cross-shard flexibility.
fs: A foreign key blocks orphan rows and drives ON DELETE behavior; it costs a parent-lookup per write, so index the parent key and weigh dropping FKs only at shard scale.
fj: Skipping foreign keys for speed and then accumulating orphaned child rows that point at parents that no longer exist.

code.label: FK with cascade
lines: -- orders; insert of bad user_id fails

q: A query filters and sorts on two columns; how does composite index column order change the plan?
a: A composite index is sorted by its columns left to right, so order is not interchangeable. An index on (a, b) serves WHERE a = ? AND b = ?, WHERE a = ? alone, and a leading prefix, but it cannot efficiently serve WHERE b = ? by itself because b is only sorted within each a. It can also satisfy ORDER BY a, b and a WHERE a = ? ... ORDER BY b without a separate sort. The guideline is equality columns first, then the range/sort column last, matching the query's most selective leading predicates.
fs: A composite (a,b) index covers leading-prefix predicates and ORDER BY a,b, but not a standalone filter on b; put equality columns first, the range/sort column last.
fj: Assuming an index on (a, b) is just as good for querying on b alone as a dedicated index on b.

code.label: Prefix rule
lines: CREATE INDEX idx ON t (a, b);
